title: Matrix encoding method in variational algorithm of calculating eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues
abstract: We propose a variational method for constructing the eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues for an arbitrary $N\times N$ complex matrix. The quantum part of our algorithm is based on encoding the matrix elements into the pure state of a quantum system and expressing the loss function with optimization parameters in terms of certain probability amplitudes in the superposition state. The principal step of this algorithm is the measurement of the ancilla state   that removes all extra terms from the above superposition and allows to probabilistically construct the required loss function along with its derivatives with respect to the optimization parameters. These output data are used to find the new values of optimization parameters for the next iteration of the loss function in the gradient optimization method. The depth and size of the circuit for this algorithm are, respectively, $O(N^2 \log N)$ and $O(\log N)$.
summary: This paper introduces a new variational quantum algorithm for computing eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues of any complex matrix. By encoding matrix elements directly into quantum amplitudes and using ancilla measurements to clean the state, it avoids the need for complex Hermitian embedding or Trotterization. The method provides a scalable way to handle non-unitary problems in quantum computing.
main_problem: Developing a variational quantum algorithm to calculate both standard eigenvalues and generalized eigenvalues for arbitrary N x N complex matrices, specifically addressing the non-unitarity obstacle.
main_result: The proposed algorithm achieves a loss function minimum of zero when matrices are transformed into upper triangular forms, with a circuit depth of O(N^2 log N) and a proven quadratic relationship between loss function accuracy and eigenvalue accuracy.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical variational approach using matrix encoding into probability amplitudes, ancilla measurement for garbage removal, and classical gradient optimization via parameter shifts.
model_or_system: A quantum computing architecture utilizing n-qubit subsystems (R, C, L, chi, psi, etc.) to encode matrix elements and perform unitary transformations.
key_observables: The loss function (L) representing the deviation from upper triangular form, the normalization factor (G), and the success probability (p) of the measurement step.
important_parameters: Matrix dimension N, circuit depth O(N^2 log N), circuit size O(log N), and the relationship between eigenvalue accuracy (epsilon) and parameter encoding accuracy (sigma).
main_assumptions: The matrices are assumed to be non-degenerate and complex; numerical simulations assume perfect encoding of optimization parameters to establish baseline performance.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the quantum circuit architecture and specific gate implementations; Figure 2 demonstrates the linear scaling of the iteration number with log(epsilon) and the quadratic scaling of the loss function accuracy.
paper_structure: The paper proposes the GEV algorithm, discusses the EV algorithm, analyzes algorithm characteristics and accuracy, and concludes with numerical simulations for 4x4 matrices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2: (a) The iteration number Nr and (b) the accuracy of the loss function εL as functions of the accuracy log ε of constructed GEV and EV. These figures show that both Nr and log εL are proportional to log ε for small enough ε. The red circle and blue squares correspond, respectively, to the problem on GEV discussed in Sec.II, and to the problem on EV discussed in Sec.III. Slopes of both lines in plate (b) are very close to each other. The algorithm for constructing EV converges faster (iteration number is less)

Fig.3a demonstrates that the convergence rate is significantly larger in the case of algorithm for constructing EV. We emphasize that the slopes of the lines approximating the sets of red circles and blue squares in Fig.3b,c are wery close to each other that confirms Eq.(50) and Eq.(51) which hold for both algorithms.

FIG. 3: (a) The iteration number Nr, (b) the accuracy of the loss function log εL and (c) the minimal reachable accuracy of eigenvalue log ε(min) as functions of the accuracy of encoding the optimization parameters log σ for both problems on GEV and EV. These figures show that all presented quantities are proportional to log σ for small enough σ. The red circles and blue squares correspond, respectively, to the problem on GEV discussed in Sec.II, and to the problem on EV discussed in Sec.III. Slopes of both lines in plates (b) and (c) are very close to each other. Similar to Fig.2, the algorithm for constructing EV converges faster.
